records.ety: [OF. descant, deschant, F. dchant, discant, LL. discantus, fr. L. dis + cantus singing, melody, fr. canere to sing. See Chant, and cf. Descant, v. i., Discant.]

defs: Originally, a double song; a melody or counterpoint sung above the plain song of the tenor; a variation of an air; a variation by ornament of the main subject or plain song.
The upper voice in part music.
The canto, cantus, or soprano voice; the treble.
quotes: Twenty doctors expound one text twenty ways, as children make descant upon plain song.
She [the nightingale] all night long her amorous descant sung.
notes: ☞ The term has also been used synonymously with counterpoint, or polyphony, which developed out of the French dchant, of the 12th century.

defs: A discourse formed on its theme, like variations on a musical air; a comment or comments.
quotes: Upon that simplest of themes how magnificent a descant!

records.ety: [From descant; n.; or directly fr. OF. descanter, deschanter; L. dis- + cantare to sing.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Descanted; p. pr. & vb. n. Descanting.]

defs: To sing a variation or accomplishment.
To comment freely; to discourse with fullness and particularity; to discourse at large.
quotes: A virtuous man should be pleased to find people descanting on his actions.
